enrichment.short_description: BetterDiscord is a free, open-source client modification for the Discord desktop app that adds custom themes, plugins, and interface tweaks not offered by the official client.
enrichment.seo_title: BetterDiscord: Custom Themes and Plugins for Discord
enrichment.seo_meta_description: BetterDiscord adds free themes, plugins, and UI customization to the Discord desktop app. See features, install steps, risks, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: BetterDiscord is a free, open-source client modification for the Discord desktop application that lets users install custom CSS themes and JavaScript plugins on top of the official Discord client. It was originally created by a developer known as Jiiks in August 2015. After the original project went dormant, a developer known as Zerebos forked it in October 2018 as BandagedBD to keep it maintained, and a full rewrite begun in March 2020 became the current BetterDiscord codebase that the project ships today under the BetterDiscord organization on GitHub. The tool works by injecting a loader into the local Discord app files rather than modifying Discord's servers, giving users a built-in plugin API, a live CSS editor, and support for third-party emote services such as BTTV and FFZ. Because BetterDiscord is entirely client-side and free, there is no company, pricing tier, or paid edition; the software and its themes and plugins are distributed for free through betterdiscord.app and community repositories, and the source code is publicly available on GitHub. A factual point worth noting: modifying the Discord client in this way is against Discord's Terms of Service, and Discord has stated publicly that client mods like BetterDiscord are not permitted, since they alter client behavior outside what Discord officially supports. In practice, enforcement has historically focused on accounts that use mods for abusive behavior, spam, or automation rather than banning every installation on sight, but using BetterDiscord always carries some risk of account action because it violates the ToS, and users should weigh that risk themselves rather than assume it is either completely safe or certain to cause a ban. BetterDiscord is popular mainly with power users, server moderators, and developers who want a more customizable or utility-rich chat experience than the stock Discord app provides. It requires manual installation and periodic updates to stay compatible after Discord ships client updates, and quality and safety of third-party plugins vary since they are community-submitted rather than reviewed by Discord itself.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is BetterDiscord
paragraphs: BetterDiscord is a free, open-source modification for the official Discord desktop client. Rather than being a separate app, it injects a loader into the existing Discord installation, giving users access to custom themes, community and self-made plugins, and interface options that Discord does not officially offer.

The project traces back to August 2015, when a developer known as Jiiks first built BetterDiscord. After periods of dormancy and a fork called BandagedBD maintained by a developer known as Zerebos, a rewrite started in 2020 became the codebase that ships today under the BetterDiscord GitHub organization.

Because it is entirely client-side and open source, anyone can inspect, modify, or contribute to the code. There is no company behind BetterDiscord and no paid tier; it is maintained by volunteer contributors and funded informally through donations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: BetterDiscord's two main pillars are themes and plugins. Themes use CSS to restyle the entire Discord interface, including support for transparent or fully custom layouts, while plugins use JavaScript through a built-in plugin API to add new behavior, such as message utilities, status automation, or voice message sending.

The app also ships with a live CSS editor for real-time style debugging, developer-facing utility classes, and compatibility with third-party emote services like BTTV and FFZ so users can send extra emotes to other BetterDiscord users.

Because themes and plugins are community-submitted, quality and safety vary. BetterDiscord itself does not review third-party plugin code for malicious behavior, so users are expected to install plugins from sources they trust.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: BetterDiscord is completely free to download and use, and it will remain free since it is an open-source community project rather than a commercial product.

There are no subscription tiers, paid plugins sold by the core project, or in-app purchases. Some individual theme or plugin authors accept optional donations, but this is separate from BetterDiscord itself.

The only cost to consider is not financial but the standing-service risk: because BetterDiscord violates Discord's Terms of Service, using it carries some risk of account action from Discord, which functions as the real trade-off against its zero monetary price.

features.title: Custom Themes
features.description: Restyle the entire Discord client with CSS-based themes, including support for transparent windows and community-made theme libraries.

features.title: Plugin System
features.description: A built-in plugin loader and JavaScript plugin API let users add new functionality, from message utilities to automation of status changes.

features.title: Live CSS Editor
features.description: Edit and preview CSS changes in real time without restarting the client, useful for building or debugging themes.

features.title: Third-Party Emote Support
features.description: Use BTTV and FFZ emotes inside Discord chat when messaging other BetterDiscord users.

features.title: Developer Utilities
features.description: Exposes utility classes and a documented plugin API so developers can build and share their own plugins.

features.title: Community Theme and Plugin Directories
features.description: Official betterdiscord.app listings let users browse and one-click install community themes and plugins.

features.title: Cross-Platform Client Support
features.description: Works on the Windows, macOS, and Linux versions of the official Discord desktop app.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers
Large, active community of theme and plugin authors
Deep interface customization beyond what official Discord offers
Source code is publicly auditable on GitHub
Built-in tools like the live CSS editor make theme development easier
cons: Violates Discord's Terms of Service, which carries some risk of account action
Not officially supported or endorsed by Discord
Requires manual reinstallation or updates after some Discord client updates
Plugin and theme quality and security vary since they are not reviewed by Discord
Only works on desktop, not on Discord's mobile or web apps

faqs.question: Is BetterDiscord free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. BetterDiscord is free and open source, with no subscription or paid tier for the core software.

faqs.question: Is BetterDiscord against Discord's Terms of Service?
faqs.answer: Yes. Discord has publicly stated that client modifications like BetterDiscord are against its Terms of Service because they alter the client outside of what Discord officially supports.

faqs.question: Will I get banned for using BetterDiscord?
faqs.answer: Using BetterDiscord carries some risk of account action since it violates the Terms of Service, though enforcement has historically focused more on accounts using it for spam, automation, or abusive plugins rather than every installation. There is no guarantee either way.

faqs.question: How do I install BetterDiscord?
faqs.answer: Users download the official installer from betterdiscord.app, which patches the local Discord client files to add the BetterDiscord loader, themes menu, and plugin menu.

faqs.question: Does BetterDiscord work on mobile or the Discord web app?
faqs.answer: No. BetterDiscord only works with the official Discord desktop application on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

faqs.question: What is the difference between BetterDiscord and Vencord?
faqs.answer: Both are Discord client mods with themes and plugins; Vencord is a separate, newer project with a different codebase and plugin format, and some tools bridge compatibility between the two ecosystems.

faqs.question: Who maintains BetterDiscord today?
faqs.answer: The current codebase, maintained under the BetterDiscord GitHub organization, descends from a rewrite started by a developer known as Zerebos after earlier versions by the original creator, Jiiks, went dormant.

faqs.question: Can I write my own BetterDiscord plugin?
faqs.answer: Yes. BetterDiscord exposes a documented plugin API and developer utility classes so anyone comfortable with JavaScript can build custom plugins.

use_cases.title: Personal Discord Customization
use_cases.description: Regular users install themes and plugins to change Discord's look and add small quality-of-life features.

use_cases.title: Server Utility Enhancements
use_cases.description: Server moderators and power users add plugins that provide extra client-side utilities not built into Discord.

use_cases.title: Plugin and Theme Development
use_cases.description: Developers use the plugin API and live CSS editor to build and share their own themes or plugins with the community.

enrichment.short_description: Betterment is a New York-based robo-advisor and SEC-registered investment advisor offering automated investing, retirement accounts, and cash management for individual investors.
enrichment.headquarters: New York City, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Betterment Review: Robo-Advisor Fees and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Betterment is a robo-advisor managing over $70 billion for over 1 million customers. See fees, features, plans, and how it works.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Betterment is an automated investing platform and SEC-registered investment advisor founded in 2008 by Jon Stein and Eli Broverman, and headquartered in New York City. It was one of the first robo-advisors in the United States and popularized automated, algorithm-driven portfolio management for retail investors. Betterment LLC is registered as an investment advisor with the SEC, while its affiliate Betterment Securities operates as a FINRA-member broker-dealer. As of mid-2026, Betterment reports managing more than 70 billion dollars in assets under management on behalf of over one million customers, up from roughly 45 billion dollars in 2023, reflecting continued growth including acquisitions such as Makara (cryptocurrency investing, 2022), Wealthsimple's US advisory accounts (2021), Marcus Invest from Goldman Sachs (2024), and Ellevest's automated investing business (2025). Sarah Levy has served as CEO since December 2020. Betterment's core product is a taxable and retirement automated investing account built from diversified portfolios of low-cost ETFs, with features like automatic rebalancing, tax-loss harvesting, and tax-coordinated portfolios that place tax-inefficient assets in tax-advantaged accounts. It offers two main consumer plans: Digital, which charges 0.25 percent of assets annually (or a flat 4 dollars per month for balances under 20,000 dollars, with no account minimum), and Premium, which charges 0.65 percent annually, requires a 100,000 dollar minimum, and adds unlimited access to licensed human financial advisors. Beyond individual investing, Betterment also offers Betterment Checking and a high-yield cash reserve account, IRAs (traditional, Roth, and SEP), and Betterment at Work, a platform for employer-sponsored 401(k) retirement plans. In January 2026, the company disclosed a data breach that exposed some customers' names, contact information, and dates of birth, alongside a wave of related fraudulent crypto scam notifications targeting customers; this is a factual, publicly reported incident rather than an ongoing feature of the product. Betterment competes primarily with other robo-advisors such as Wealthfront and SoFi, as well as traditional brokerages offering automated investing add-ons.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Betterment
paragraphs: Betterment is an automated investing platform, founded in 2008 by Jon Stein and Eli Broverman and headquartered in New York City. It is widely credited as one of the earliest robo-advisors to bring algorithm-driven portfolio management to everyday retail investors.

Betterment LLC operates as a registered investment advisor with the SEC, and its affiliate Betterment Securities is a FINRA-member broker-dealer, which together provide the regulatory basis for holding and managing customer investment accounts.

As of mid-2026, Betterment manages more than 70 billion dollars in assets for over one million customers, spanning individual taxable accounts, retirement accounts, cash accounts, and employer-sponsored retirement plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Betterment builds diversified portfolios out of low-cost ETFs and automatically rebalances them as markets move or a customer's goals change. Tax-loss harvesting and tax-coordinated portfolio placement are available to help reduce the tax drag on taxable accounts.

The platform supports multiple account types, including individual and joint taxable accounts, traditional, Roth, and SEP IRAs, a high-yield cash reserve account, and Betterment Checking. Betterment at Work extends the platform to employers offering 401(k) plans to their staff.

Following its acquisition of Makara, Betterment also offers automated cryptocurrency investing portfolios, and the Premium plan adds ongoing access to a team of licensed human financial advisors for more complex financial planning needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Betterment's Digital plan charges 0.25 percent of assets under management annually, or a flat 4 dollars per month for accounts under 20,000 dollars that do not have recurring monthly deposits set up, with no minimum balance required to open an account.

The Premium plan charges 0.65 percent of assets annually, requires a 100,000 dollar minimum balance, and adds unlimited access to Betterment's team of certified financial planners for personalized advice.

Betterment at Work and advisor-facing products (Betterment Advisor Solutions) are priced separately and are typically negotiated with employers or advisory firms rather than sold at a fixed consumer rate.

features.title: Automated Portfolio Management
features.description: Builds and continuously rebalances diversified ETF portfolios based on a customer's goals and risk tolerance.

features.title: Tax-Loss Harvesting
features.description: Automatically sells losing positions to offset gains and reduce taxable income in eligible taxable accounts.

features.title: Tax-Coordinated Portfolio
features.description: Places tax-inefficient assets into tax-advantaged accounts and tax-efficient assets into taxable accounts to reduce overall tax drag.

features.title: Human Advisor Access
features.description: Premium plan customers get unlimited access to Betterment's licensed financial advisors for planning guidance.

features.title: Cash Management
features.description: Betterment Checking and a high-yield cash reserve account let customers manage everyday spending and savings alongside investments.

features.title: Retirement Accounts
features.description: Supports traditional, Roth, and SEP IRAs as well as employer-sponsored 401(k) plans through Betterment at Work.

features.title: Automated Crypto Investing
features.description: Offers automated cryptocurrency portfolios following Betterment's acquisition of Makara.

features.title: Goal-Based Planning Tools
features.description: In-app tools help customers set and track progress toward goals like retirement, a home purchase, or an emergency fund.

pros: Lower fees than traditional human financial advisors
No account minimum on the Digital plan
Automatic rebalancing and tax-loss harvesting built in
Wide range of account types, including checking, IRAs, and employer 401(k)s
Access to licensed human advisors on the Premium plan
Long operating history and SEC/FINRA regulatory oversight
cons: Premium plan requires a 100,000 dollar minimum balance
0.65 percent Premium fee is higher than some robo-advisor competitors
Limited direct control over individual stock selection
Cash account yields fluctuate with prevailing interest rates
Disclosed a data breach in January 2026 affecting some customer contact information

faqs.question: What is Betterment?
faqs.answer: Betterment is a robo-advisor and SEC-registered investment advisor that offers automated investing, retirement accounts, and cash management for individual investors.

faqs.question: How much does Betterment cost?
faqs.answer: The Digital plan charges 0.25 percent of assets annually or 4 dollars per month for smaller balances, while the Premium plan charges 0.65 percent annually and requires a 100,000 dollar minimum.

faqs.question: Is Betterment safe and regulated?
faqs.answer: Betterment LLC is registered as an investment advisor with the SEC, and its affiliate Betterment Securities is a FINRA-member broker-dealer, so customer accounts fall under standard securities industry regulation and SIPC coverage for the brokerage accounts.

faqs.question: Does Betterment have a minimum investment?
faqs.answer: The Digital plan has no account minimum. The Premium plan requires a 100,000 dollar minimum balance.

faqs.question: What is tax-loss harvesting?
faqs.answer: It is an automated strategy where Betterment sells investments at a loss to offset taxable gains elsewhere in a customer's portfolio, potentially lowering their tax bill.

faqs.question: Can I talk to a human advisor at Betterment?
faqs.answer: Yes, unlimited access to licensed financial advisors is included with the Premium plan; Digital plan customers can purchase one-off advice packages.

faqs.question: How much does Betterment manage in assets?
faqs.answer: As of mid-2026, Betterment reports managing more than 70 billion dollars in assets for over one million customers.

faqs.question: Has Betterment had any security incidents?
faqs.answer: In January 2026, Betterment disclosed a data breach exposing some customers' names, contact information, and dates of birth, along with related fraudulent crypto scam notifications; this is a publicly reported incident rather than an ongoing feature of the platform.

use_cases.title: Hands-Off Retail Investing
use_cases.description: Investors who want a diversified, automatically managed portfolio without picking individual stocks.

use_cases.title: Retirement Savings
use_cases.description: Savers use Betterment's traditional, Roth, or SEP IRAs to build long-term retirement portfolios with automated rebalancing.

use_cases.title: Employer-Sponsored Retirement Plans
use_cases.description: Small and mid-sized businesses use Betterment at Work to offer employees a 401(k) plan without building one in-house.

pricing_plans.billing_cycle: Annual fee (AUM-based) or monthly flat fee

features: Unlimited access to licensed financial advisors

enrichment.short_description: Betterworks is an enterprise performance management platform that combines OKR goal-setting, continuous feedback, performance reviews, calibration, and talent intelligence in one system.
enrichment.seo_title: Betterworks: Enterprise OKR and Performance Management
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Betterworks combines OKRs, feedback, performance reviews, and talent intelligence for enterprises. See features, pricing, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Betterworks is an enterprise performance management and talent intelligence software platform founded in 2013 and headquartered in Menlo Park, California, with an additional office in New York City. The company was co-founded by Kris Duggan, who served as founding CEO before stepping away from day-to-day leadership in 2017. Betterworks raised venture funding from investors including Kleiner Perkins and Emergence Capital, totaling roughly 40 million dollars across its early funding rounds. The platform's core offering combines goal and OKR (objectives and key results) management with continuous feedback, structured performance reviews, calibration workflows, succession planning, skills intelligence, and AI-assisted coaching and workforce insights, positioning it as a connected performance and talent system rather than a single-purpose OKR tool. Betterworks sells three main plans: Performance, priced at 8 dollars per user per month, which covers goal and OKR tracking, feedback, review conversations, and manager 1-on-1 tools; Performance plus Talent Intelligence, priced at 9 dollars per user per month, which adds calibration, succession planning, skills intelligence, and unified talent profiles; and a custom-priced Enterprise tier that adds transformational program design, engagement and culture measurement, and a dedicated implementation team. Betterworks does not offer self-serve signup, and the company positions its platform primarily for organizations of 500 or more employees, making it best suited to mid-market and large enterprises rather than small teams. Current estimates place Betterworks at roughly 160 to 220 employees as of 2026, depending on the data source. The platform integrates with common workplace tools such as Slack, Microsoft Teams, and HRIS systems, and it competes with other performance management and OKR platforms such as Lattice, Culture Amp, and 15Five.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Betterworks
paragraphs: Betterworks is an enterprise performance management platform founded in 2013 and headquartered in Menlo Park, California. It was built around the idea of connecting OKR-based goal setting to the rest of the performance management cycle, including feedback, reviews, and talent decisions.

The platform was co-founded by Kris Duggan, who led the company as CEO in its early years before stepping away from day-to-day leadership in 2017; Betterworks has continued operating and expanding its product line since then.

Rather than functioning as a standalone OKR tracker, Betterworks combines goals and OKRs, continuous feedback, performance reviews, calibration, succession planning, and AI-powered workforce insights into a single connected system aimed at mid-market and large enterprises.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the core of Betterworks is goal and OKR management, letting organizations cascade objectives from company strategy down to team and individual goals, with dashboards to track progress in real time.

The platform layers continuous feedback, structured 1-on-1 tools, and formal performance review cycles on top of goal tracking, so managers can connect day-to-day coaching conversations to formal review outcomes.

Higher tiers add talent intelligence features such as calibration workflows for consistent rating decisions across teams, succession planning, skills intelligence, and unified talent profiles, along with AI-assisted development plans and workforce insights.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Betterworks sells three main plans, all priced per user per month: Performance, Performance plus Talent Intelligence, and a custom-quoted Enterprise tier.

The Performance plan starts at 8 dollars per user per month and covers OKR and goal tracking, feedback, review conversations, and manager 1-on-1 tools. The Performance plus Talent Intelligence plan starts at 9 dollars per user per month and adds calibration, succession planning, and skills intelligence.

The Enterprise plan is custom-priced and adds transformational program design, engagement and culture measurement, and a dedicated implementation team. There is no self-serve signup, and Betterworks positions its platform primarily for organizations with 500 or more employees.

features.title: OKR and Goal Management
features.description: Cascade company objectives down to team and individual OKRs with real-time progress dashboards.

features.title: Continuous Feedback
features.description: Structured tools for ongoing, real-time feedback between managers and employees outside of formal review cycles.

features.title: Performance Reviews
features.description: Configurable review cycles and conversation tools that tie back to goal progress and feedback history.

features.title: Calibration
features.description: Workflows that help managers and HR align performance ratings consistently across teams and departments.

features.title: Succession Planning
features.description: Tools for identifying and tracking succession candidates for key roles across the organization.

features.title: Skills Intelligence
features.description: AI-assisted analysis of employee skills to support development planning and workforce decisions.

features.title: Manager 1-on-1 Tools
features.description: Built-in templates and tracking for recurring manager-employee check-in meetings.

features.title: AI-Powered Insights
features.description: AI features that generate development plan suggestions and surface workforce trends for HR and leadership.

pros: Combines OKRs, feedback, and formal reviews in one connected platform
Strong calibration and succession planning features for enterprise HR teams
AI-assisted coaching and workforce insights
Established vendor with a long operating history since 2013
Integrates with common workplace tools like Slack, Teams, and HRIS systems
cons: No self-serve signup, requiring a sales conversation for pricing
Best suited to organizations with 500 or more employees
Higher cost than lightweight, OKR-only tools
Enterprise tier pricing is fully custom and not published
Implementation and rollout can require dedicated onboarding support

faqs.question: What is Betterworks used for?
faqs.answer: Betterworks is used by enterprises to manage OKRs and goals, continuous feedback, performance reviews, calibration, and succession planning in one connected platform.

faqs.question: How much does Betterworks cost?
faqs.answer: Betterworks lists a Performance plan starting at 8 dollars per user per month and a Performance plus Talent Intelligence plan starting at 9 dollars per user per month, with a custom-priced Enterprise tier for larger organizations.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial or self-serve plan?
faqs.answer: No. Betterworks does not offer self-serve signup; pricing and access require contacting sales.

faqs.question: What size organization is Betterworks built for?
faqs.answer: Betterworks is positioned primarily for mid-market and large enterprises, with the company noting organizations of 500 or more employees see the most value.

faqs.question: When was Betterworks founded?
faqs.answer: Betterworks was founded in 2013 and is headquartered in Menlo Park, California, with an additional office in New York City.

faqs.question: Does Betterworks include performance reviews, not just OKRs?
faqs.answer: Yes. Betterworks combines OKR and goal tracking with continuous feedback, formal performance reviews, and calibration in a single platform.

faqs.question: Does Betterworks use AI?
faqs.answer: Yes. Betterworks includes AI-assisted features for coaching suggestions, development plans, and workforce insights, particularly on its Talent Intelligence and Enterprise tiers.

faqs.question: Who are Betterworks' main competitors?
faqs.answer: Betterworks competes with other performance management and OKR platforms such as Lattice, Culture Amp, and 15Five.

use_cases.title: Enterprise OKR Alignment
use_cases.description: Large organizations use Betterworks to cascade company strategy into team and individual OKRs with real-time tracking.

use_cases.title: Connected Performance Reviews
use_cases.description: HR teams tie goal progress and continuous feedback directly into structured performance review cycles.

use_cases.title: Succession and Talent Planning
use_cases.description: People analytics teams use calibration and succession planning tools to make consistent talent decisions across the company.
use_cases.audience: People analytics and talent management teams

enrichment.short_description: Bevy is a free, open-source, data-driven game engine built in Rust, known for its fast Entity Component System (ECS) architecture and modular plugin design.
enrichment.seo_title: Bevy Engine: Free Open-Source Rust Game Engine
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bevy is a free, open-source Rust game engine with a fast ECS architecture. See features, pricing, pros and cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bevy is a free and open-source game engine and application framework written in the Rust programming language. It was created by Carter Anderson, a former Microsoft software engineer, with early development starting around January 2020 and a first public announcement and release, version 0.1, on August 10, 2020. Bevy is built around a data-driven Entity Component System (ECS) architecture, in which entities are simple identifiers, components are plain data attached to those entities, and systems are functions that operate on components, allowing the engine's scheduler to run many systems in parallel for performance. The engine includes modern 2D and 3D rendering with support for sprites, cameras, lights, shadows, and glTF assets, a Bevy UI framework built on ECS principles with a flexbox-style layout model, hot-reloading of assets during development, and cross-platform support for Windows, macOS, Linux, and the web via WebAssembly. Bevy is dual-licensed under the MIT and Apache 2.0 licenses, meaning developers can choose either license and use the engine free of charge, including for commercial games, with no royalties or revenue-sharing requirements. As of the engine's ongoing development, Bevy has not yet reached a 1.0 release, and its API has continued to change between minor versions, which developers should account for when starting new projects. Development is coordinated through the open-source GitHub repository under the bevyengine organization, with contributions from a large community of volunteers, and is financially supported in part by the nonprofit Bevy Foundation, established to help fund ongoing engine development. Bevy has become one of the most widely used Rust game engines, frequently compared to alternatives such as Macroquad, ggez, and Fyrox, and is popular with developers who want a modern ECS-based architecture and the performance and memory-safety characteristics of Rust.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Bevy Engine
paragraphs: Bevy is a free, open-source game engine and application framework written entirely in Rust. It was created by developer Carter Anderson, with the project's first public release, version 0.1, launching on August 10, 2020.

The engine is described by its creators as refreshingly simple and data-driven, built around an Entity Component System (ECS) architecture rather than the object-oriented, inheritance-based design used by many older engines.

Bevy is maintained as an open-source project on GitHub under the bevyengine organization, with development supported in part by the nonprofit Bevy Foundation and a large community of volunteer contributors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Bevy's core is its ECS, where entities are lightweight IDs, components are pure data, and systems are functions that operate on that data; the scheduler runs independent systems in parallel automatically, which the project describes as massively parallel, cache-friendly, and fast.

The engine includes 2D and 3D rendering with support for sprites, cameras, lighting, shadows, and glTF model import, a native Bevy UI framework built on the same ECS principles with a flexbox-style layout model, and hot asset reloading so changes appear without restarting the app.

Bevy targets Windows, macOS, Linux, and the web through WebAssembly from a single codebase, and the project has invested heavily in fast compile times, with iterative build times reported as low as under a few seconds in optimized development configurations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Bevy is completely free and open source, dual-licensed under the MIT and Apache 2.0 licenses. Developers can choose whichever license suits their project and use the engine for personal, commercial, or open-source games without paying fees or royalties.

There is no paid tier, editor license, or revenue-sharing requirement of any kind, which distinguishes Bevy from commercial engines like Unity or Unreal Engine that have licensing fees or royalty thresholds.

Because Bevy is community-funded through the nonprofit Bevy Foundation and direct sponsorships, users who want to support ongoing development can contribute financially, but doing so is entirely optional and not required to use the engine.

features.title: Entity Component System
features.description: A parallel, cache-friendly ECS separates data (components) from behavior (systems) for modular, high-performance game logic.

features.title: 2D and 3D Rendering
features.description: Built-in rendering pipeline supports sprites, cameras, lighting, shadows, and glTF 3D model import.

features.title: Bevy UI Framework
features.description: A native, ECS-driven UI system with a flexbox-style layout model for building game and editor interfaces.

features.title: Cross-Platform Builds
features.description: Ships to Windows, macOS, Linux, and the web via WebAssembly from a single Rust codebase.

features.title: Hot Asset Reloading
features.description: Assets can be modified and reloaded while the app is running, speeding up iteration during development.

features.title: Plugin Architecture
features.description: Engine functionality is organized into modular plugins that can be added, removed, or replaced by third-party crates.

features.title: Fast Compile Times
features.description: The project prioritizes short iterative compile times relative to other Rust game development setups.

features.title: Open-Source, Dual-Licensed Code
features.description: Distributed under the MIT or Apache 2.0 license, free for personal and commercial use with no royalties.

pros: Completely free and open source under MIT or Apache 2.0 licensing
Fast, parallel ECS architecture designed for performance
Actively developed with frequent releases and a large community
Memory-safe by virtue of being built in Rust
Growing ecosystem of third-party plugin crates
Backed in part by the nonprofit Bevy Foundation
cons: Still pre-1.0, with breaking API changes between minor versions
Smaller ecosystem and asset marketplace than Unity, Unreal, or Godot
Requires knowledge of Rust, which has a steeper learning curve than some scripting languages
No full built-in visual editor comparable to Unity or Unreal's editors
Documentation and tutorials can lag behind the engine's fast-moving API

faqs.question: Is Bevy Engine free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bevy is free and open source under the MIT and Apache 2.0 licenses, with no fees or royalties for personal or commercial use.

faqs.question: What programming language is Bevy written in?
faqs.answer: Bevy is written in Rust, and games built with it are also written in Rust.

faqs.question: Does Bevy support both 2D and 3D games?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bevy includes rendering support for both 2D sprites and 3D scenes with lighting, shadows, and glTF model import.

faqs.question: Is Bevy production ready?
faqs.answer: Bevy is actively used for shipped games and tools, but as of its ongoing development it has not reached a 1.0 release, and its API can change between versions, which teams should plan for.

faqs.question: What license does Bevy use?
faqs.answer: Bevy is dual-licensed under the MIT and Apache 2.0 licenses, letting developers choose either license.

faqs.question: How does Bevy compare to Unity or Godot?
faqs.answer: Bevy uses Rust and a code-first ECS architecture rather than a visual editor-first workflow like Unity or Godot, and it has a smaller asset ecosystem but offers Rust's performance and memory safety.

faqs.question: Does Bevy have a visual editor?
faqs.answer: Bevy does not yet ship a full built-in visual editor comparable to Unity or Unreal, though editor tooling is an active area of community development.

faqs.question: Who created Bevy Engine?
faqs.answer: Bevy was created by developer Carter Anderson, who first released it publicly in August 2020, and it is now maintained by a broad open-source community.

use_cases.title: Indie Game Development
use_cases.description: Independent and hobbyist developers use Bevy to build 2D and 3D games with a modern ECS architecture at no licensing cost.

use_cases.title: Rust Application Development
use_cases.description: Rust developers use Bevy's ECS and rendering framework for interactive simulations and applications beyond traditional games.

use_cases.title: Open-Source Game Projects
use_cases.description: Open-source game projects choose Bevy for its permissive licensing and transparent, community-driven development process.

enrichment.short_description: Bexi was a San Francisco-based design-subscription platform that gave marketing teams on-demand access to a creative production team for video, digital ads, and web design work.
enrichment.seo_title: Bexi: Design Subscription Platform for Marketing Teams
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bexi offered points-based design subscriptions for marketing teams needing video, ad, and web design work without hiring in-house.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bexi was launched in August 2016 by Rafael Gomez and Cuco Vega and was based in San Francisco, with additional production offices in Mexico, including Guadalajara, Mexico City, and La Paz. It operated as a design-subscription and creative-operations platform aimed at marketing teams that needed a steady stream of design output, such as video content, digital advertisements, and website design, without hiring additional in-house designers or paying traditional agency rates. Bexi's model was built around monthly or annual subscription plans that granted customers a pool of points, redeemable for standard or premium design assets and managed through a dedicated account manager who helped clients prioritize and open projects; unused points rolled over to the following month. Two plan tiers referenced in public sources were called Flexi 10 and Flexi 40 Plus, aimed respectively at customers needing a steady but modest volume of assets for one product or offering, and customers managing a higher volume of campaigns across multiple products or brands. According to third-party company data sources, Bexi reported around 1.3 million dollars in revenue with a roughly 10-person team as of 2023, and received investment from Magma Partners. As of this research, however, the bexi.io domain redirects to an unrelated personal website rather than showing an active product or marketing site, and a pricing page on the same domain displays only unpopulated template text, which suggests the original Bexi platform as described here may no longer be operating in its previous form, or that the company's web presence has changed. Because independent, current confirmation of Bexi's operating status was not found, this summary should be read as a description of the historical Bexi design-subscription business based on the information available, not a claim about its present-day availability. Prospective customers or researchers should verify current status directly before relying on Bexi as an active vendor.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Bexi
paragraphs: Bexi was a design-subscription platform launched in August 2016 by Rafael Gomez and Cuco Vega, based in San Francisco with production offices in Mexico. It positioned itself as an alternative to hiring in-house designers or paying traditional agency retainers for marketing creative work.

The service targeted marketing teams at small and mid-sized businesses that needed an ongoing, flexible supply of design assets, such as digital ads, video content, and website design, without the overhead of building an internal creative team.

As of this research, the bexi.io domain no longer displays an active product site; it redirects to an unrelated personal website, and a pricing page on the domain shows unpopulated template text rather than published pricing, so the current operating status of the business is unclear from public sources.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Bexi's model centered on a points-based subscription: customers received a monthly allotment of points that could be redeemed for standard or premium design deliverables, giving them flexibility to request different types of assets within a single plan.

Each account was paired with a dedicated account manager who helped clients scope, prioritize, and open new design projects, functioning as the main point of contact between the customer and Bexi's production team.

Because unused points rolled over to the following month, customers could bank capacity during slower periods and use it during higher-volume campaign pushes, which was positioned as more flexible than a fixed-scope agency retainer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Bexi offered at least two named subscription tiers, Flexi 10 and Flexi 40 Plus, differentiated by the volume of design points included each month, with Flexi 40 Plus aimed at customers managing multiple products or a higher volume of campaigns.
Plans required an annual commitment but gave customers the choice of paying monthly or paying upfront for a reported discount, according to third-party pricing pages associated with the Bexi brand.

Exact dollar pricing for each tier was not published in the sources available for this research, and the current pricing page on bexi.io does not display live pricing, so prospective customers should contact the company directly to confirm current rates and availability.

features.title: Points-Based Subscription
features.description: Customers received a monthly allotment of points redeemable for standard or premium design deliverables.

features.title: Dedicated Account Manager
features.description: Each subscription included a named account manager who helped scope and prioritize design requests.

features.title: Points Rollover
features.description: Unused monthly points carried over, letting customers bank capacity for busier campaign periods.

features.title: Video Content Production
features.description: Design output included video assets for marketing and advertising campaigns.

features.title: Digital Advertisement Design
features.description: Produced digital ad creative for marketing teams running paid campaigns.

features.title: Website Design
features.description: Offered website design services as part of its broader creative deliverable catalog.

features.title: Tiered Subscription Plans
features.description: Offered Flexi 10 and Flexi 40 Plus tiers scaled to different volumes of monthly design output.

pros: Avoided the cost of hiring and managing an in-house design team
Points system allowed flexibility across different design deliverable types
Dedicated account manager simplified project intake
Unused points rolled over instead of being forfeited
Covered a broad range of deliverables, including video, ads, and web design
cons: Exact subscription pricing was not publicly published
Current operating status is unclear based on available public information
The primary bexi.io website no longer displays an active product presence as of this research
Independent, recent customer reviews of the platform are limited
Required an annual commitment even when paying monthly

faqs.question: What did Bexi do?
faqs.answer: Bexi was a design-subscription platform that gave marketing teams on-demand access to a creative production team for deliverables like video content, digital ads, and website design, in exchange for a monthly or annual subscription.

faqs.question: Who founded Bexi?
faqs.answer: Bexi was founded in August 2016 by Rafael Gomez and Cuco Vega, and was based in San Francisco with production offices in Mexico.

faqs.question: Is Bexi still active?
faqs.answer: Based on this research, the bexi.io domain currently redirects to an unrelated personal website and its pricing page shows unpopulated template text, so its current operating status could not be confirmed from public sources.

faqs.question: How did the Flexi points system work?
faqs.answer: Customers received a set number of points each month based on their plan tier, redeemable for standard or premium design assets, with unused points rolling over to future months.

faqs.question: What kind of design work did Bexi handle?
faqs.answer: Bexi's team produced video content, digital advertisements, and website design for marketing teams, based on publicly available descriptions of the service.

faqs.question: Did Bexi publish self-serve pricing?
faqs.answer: No specific dollar pricing for the Flexi 10 or Flexi 40 Plus plans was found in public sources during this research; customers reportedly worked with an account manager to select a plan.

use_cases.title: Marketing Team Creative Support
use_cases.description: Marketing teams used Bexi to outsource ongoing design production without hiring additional in-house designers.

use_cases.description: Agencies reportedly used services like Bexi to add creative production capacity for client campaigns without hiring more staff.

enrichment.short_description: bibisco is novel-writing software that helps fiction authors organize characters, plot, chapters, scenes, and locations in a single desktop writing workspace.
enrichment.seo_title: bibisco: Novel Writing Software for Fiction Authors
enrichment.seo_meta_description: bibisco helps novelists plan characters, plot, and chapters in a free desktop app. See features, pricing, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: bibisco is novel-writing software created by Italian developer Andrea Feccomandi, who has worked on the project as founder since around 2012 and is based in Bologna, Italy. It is a desktop application, available for Windows, macOS, and Linux, designed specifically for planning and writing long-form fiction such as novels. The software organizes a project around several core planning tools: a character development section that uses a structured character interview approach and tracks character arcs over the course of the story, a plot and structure tool for organizing a novel into parts, chapters, and scenes before drafting begins, a distraction-focused word processor with formatting and spell-check tailored to fiction writing, a story analysis feature that reports statistics on novel length, point-of-view distribution, and character and location usage, and mind maps that visually show relationships and connections between characters, locations, objects, and groups to help authors catch plot holes. bibisco is distributed under a freemium model: the Community Edition is free and fully functional for planning and writing a novel, while the Supporter Edition costs 23 euros as a one-time purchase and adds custom themes and more advanced analysis features; a 20 percent discount is available for students and educators who provide enrollment documentation. According to bibisco's own materials, the software has been downloaded more than 200,000 times and has been translated into more than 15 languages. bibisco is commonly compared to other novel-planning and writing tools such as Scrivener, though it is distinguished by being free at its core, open in its development approach, and built specifically around structured pre-writing planning tools like character interviews and mind maps rather than functioning primarily as a general-purpose long-document word processor.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is bibisco
paragraphs: bibisco is a desktop novel-writing application created by Italian developer Andrea Feccomandi, who has led the project as founder since around 2012 from Bologna, Italy. It is built specifically for fiction authors who want to plan and write a novel within a single workspace.

Unlike a general word processor, bibisco combines a dedicated planning layer, covering characters, plot structure, and locations, with a writing-focused text editor, so authors can move between outlining and drafting in the same application.
The software runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux and, according to its own materials, has been downloaded more than 200,000 times and translated into more than 15 languages.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: bibisco's character tools include a structured character interview process meant to help authors develop each character in depth, along with sections for tracking how a character's arc evolves across the story.

For structure, authors organize their novel into parts, chapters, and scenes before writing, and can use bibisco's mind maps to visually map relationships between characters, locations, objects, and groups, which is intended to help catch plot holes and inconsistencies early.

A story analysis feature reports statistics on the novel's length, point-of-view distribution, and how characters and locations are used across the manuscript, while the built-in word processor includes formatting tools, spell check, and dialogue-formatting shortcuts aimed at fiction writers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: bibisco follows a freemium model with two editions. The Community Edition is free and provides a fully functional set of planning and writing tools for an entire novel.

The Supporter Edition costs 23 euros as a one-time purchase rather than a subscription, and adds custom themes along with more advanced story analysis features on top of everything in the Community Edition.
Students and educators can receive a 20 percent discount on the Supporter Edition by emailing bibisco with valid enrollment documentation or a school certificate.

features.title: Character Interview Tool
features.description: A structured interview process helps authors develop each character in depth before and during writing.

features.title: Character Arc Tracking
features.description: Dedicated sections track how each character evolves across the course of the novel.

features.title: Plot and Chapter Structure
features.description: Organize a novel into parts, chapters, and scenes to build a clear outline before drafting.

features.title: Distraction-Focused Word Processor
features.description: A writer-oriented text editor with formatting tools, spell check, and dialogue-formatting shortcuts.

features.title: Story Analysis Reports
features.description: Generates statistics on novel length, point-of-view distribution, and character and location usage.

features.title: Mind Maps
features.description: Visual maps show relationships and connections between characters, locations, objects, and groups.

features.title: Cross-Platform Desktop App
features.description: Available for Windows, macOS, and Linux as a native desktop application.

features.title: Multi-Language Support
features.description: Translated into more than 15 languages according to bibisco's own materials.

pros: Community Edition is free and fully functional for planning and writing a novel
Structured planning tools suit writers who like to outline before drafting
Runs offline as a native desktop app, keeping manuscripts private
Mind maps and story analysis are less common in competing writing tools
One-time payment for the Supporter Edition instead of a subscription
Available in more than 15 languages
cons: Interface is less polished than some premium competitors like Scrivener
Advanced themes and analysis features require the paid Supporter Edition
No built-in cloud sync or real-time collaboration features
Structured planning approach may not suit pantser writers who prefer to write without outlining first
Desktop-only, with no official web or mobile app

faqs.question: Is bibisco free?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Community Edition of bibisco is free and provides a fully functional set of tools for planning and writing a novel.

faqs.question: What does the Supporter Edition add?
faqs.answer: The 23 euro one-time Supporter Edition adds custom themes and more advanced story analysis features on top of the free Community Edition.

faqs.question: Does bibisco work offline?
faqs.answer: Yes. bibisco is a native desktop application that runs offline on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

faqs.question: Is there a Mac and Linux version of bibisco?
faqs.answer: Yes. bibisco is available for Windows, macOS, and Linux.

faqs.question: Does bibisco support collaboration with co-authors?
faqs.answer: bibisco is designed as a single-user desktop application and does not include built-in real-time collaboration features.

faqs.question: Can students get a discount on bibisco?
faqs.answer: Yes. Students and educators can get a 20 percent discount on the Supporter Edition by emailing bibisco with valid enrollment documentation.

faqs.question: Who created bibisco?
faqs.answer: bibisco was created by Italian developer Andrea Feccomandi, who has led the project since around 2012 from Bologna, Italy.

faqs.question: How is bibisco different from Scrivener?
faqs.answer: bibisco is free at its core and centers on structured pre-writing planning tools like character interviews and mind maps, while Scrivener is a paid, general-purpose long-document writing tool with a broader feature set.

use_cases.title: Multi-Book Series Planning
use_cases.description: Novelists use bibisco's character and plot tools to keep details consistent across a series of books.

use_cases.title: Structured Outlining
use_cases.description: Writers who prefer to plan chapters, scenes, and character arcs before drafting use bibisco as their planning hub.

use_cases.title: Free Offline Writing Tool
use_cases.description: Students and self-published authors use the free Community Edition as a private, offline alternative to paid writing software.

enrichment.short_description: BibleTime is a free, open-source Bible study application built on the CrossWire SWORD Project library, offering Bibles, commentaries, dictionaries, and books for Linux, Windows, and macOS.
enrichment.seo_title: BibleTime: Free Open-Source Bible Study Software
enrichment.seo_meta_description: BibleTime is a free, open-source Bible study app built on the SWORD Project. Access 200+ texts on Linux, Windows, and macOS.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: BibleTime is a free, open-source Bible study application written in C++ using the Qt GUI toolkit, with development tracing back to at least 2006. It is built on top of the SWORD Project, a cross-platform library maintained by the CrossWire Bible Society that provides programmatic access, search, and utility functions for a large collection of digitized Bible texts, commentaries, dictionaries, and other reference books. Through the SWORD library, BibleTime gives users access to more than 200 free texts covering more than 50 languages, including public-domain and freely licensed Bible translations, commentaries, and lexicons distributed by CrossWire and affiliated Bible societies. The application is licensed under the GNU General Public License (GPL), consistent with the SWORD Project's own open-source licensing, and is available as a native desktop program for Linux, Windows, and macOS, as well as FreeBSD. Core features include a parallel view for comparing multiple Bible translations side by side, full-text search across installed modules, the ability to write and save personal notes and highlights, and a built-in module manager for browsing and downloading additional texts directly from CrossWire's repositories. Because BibleTime works with locally downloaded SWORD modules, it can be used fully offline once the desired texts have been installed. BibleTime is maintained by a community of volunteer developers affiliated with the CrossWire Bible Society rather than a commercial company, and its source code is hosted publicly on GitHub under the BibleTime organization. It is one of several SWORD Project front-end applications, alongside others built for different platforms, all sharing the same underlying library and module ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is BibleTime
paragraphs: BibleTime is a free, open-source Bible study application built in C++ with the Qt GUI toolkit. Its development dates back to at least 2006, and it remains one of the primary desktop front ends for the CrossWire Bible Society's SWORD Project.

Rather than shipping its own proprietary text library, BibleTime uses the SWORD library to access a shared collection of more than 200 free Bible texts, commentaries, dictionaries, and books covering more than 50 languages, maintained by CrossWire and affiliated Bible societies.

The application is licensed under the GNU General Public License and is available for Linux, Windows, macOS, and FreeBSD, maintained by a community of volunteer developers rather than a commercial company.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: BibleTime lets users open multiple Bible translations, commentaries, and reference books side by side for comparative study, and includes full-text search across all installed modules to locate verses, terms, or topics quickly.
Users can write and save personal notes and highlights directly within the application, and print or export study material as needed.
A built-in module manager connects to CrossWire's repositories, letting users browse, download, and install additional Bible texts, commentaries, and dictionaries directly from within BibleTime.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: BibleTime is completely free to download and use, consistent with the CrossWire Bible Society's mission of providing free, open-source Bible software.
There are no subscription fees, in-app purchases, or paid tiers; the application and the vast majority of texts distributed through the SWORD module system are free.
Because BibleTime is open source under the GPL, its source code can also be freely inspected, modified, and redistributed by anyone.

features.title: Access to 200+ SWORD Texts
features.description: Connects to the CrossWire SWORD library for free access to Bibles, commentaries, dictionaries, and books in more than 50 languages.

features.title: Parallel Bible View
features.description: Displays multiple translations or reference works side by side for comparative study.

features.title: Full-Text Search
features.description: Search across all installed modules to quickly find verses, terms, or topics.

features.title: Personal Notes and Highlights
features.description: Write, save, and organize personal study notes and highlighted passages within the app.

features.title: Module Manager
features.description: Browse and download additional Bible texts and reference works directly from CrossWire's repositories.

features.title: Offline Study
features.description: Works fully offline once desired SWORD modules are downloaded and installed.

features.title: Cross-Platform Support
features.description: Native builds available for Linux, Windows, macOS, and FreeBSD.

features.title: Open-Source GPL Codebase
features.description: Source code is publicly available and licensed under the GNU General Public License.

pros: Completely free with no ads or subscription requirements
Large open library of Bible texts, commentaries, and dictionaries
Works fully offline once modules are downloaded
Actively maintained by the CrossWire open-source community
Available on Linux, Windows, and macOS
No account creation or sign-in required
cons: Interface feels dated compared to modern commercial Bible apps
No official BibleTime mobile app, though other SWORD front ends exist for mobile
Module installation and management can be confusing for new users
Available texts depend on what CrossWire and partner societies have digitized
Development pace is slower than well-funded commercial Bible software

faqs.question: Is BibleTime free?
faqs.answer: Yes. BibleTime is free, open-source software with no subscription fees or paid tiers.

faqs.question: What is the SWORD Project?
faqs.answer: The SWORD Project is the CrossWire Bible Society's open-source library that provides access, search, and utility functions for a large collection of digitized Bible texts, which BibleTime uses as its content engine.

faqs.question: How do I add more Bible translations to BibleTime?
faqs.answer: BibleTime includes a built-in module manager that lets users browse and download additional texts, commentaries, and dictionaries from CrossWire's repositories.

faqs.question: Does BibleTime work offline?
faqs.answer: Yes. Once the desired SWORD modules are downloaded, BibleTime can be used fully offline.

faqs.question: Is there a BibleTime app for iPhone or Android?
faqs.answer: BibleTime itself is a desktop application for Linux, Windows, and macOS; other SWORD Project front ends are built separately for mobile platforms.

faqs.question: What license does BibleTime use?
faqs.answer: BibleTime is licensed under the GNU General Public License (GPL), consistent with the SWORD Project's open-source licensing.

faqs.question: Is BibleTime open source?
faqs.answer: Yes. Its source code is publicly hosted on GitHub under the BibleTime organization and licensed under the GPL.

faqs.question: How many texts does BibleTime give access to?
faqs.answer: Through the SWORD library, BibleTime provides access to more than 200 free texts covering more than 50 languages, including Bibles, commentaries, and dictionaries.

use_cases.title: Comparative Bible Study
use_cases.description: Students and pastors use BibleTime's parallel view and search to compare translations and commentaries side by side.

use_cases.title: Offline, Private Study
use_cases.description: Users who want a private, ad-free, offline Bible study tool install BibleTime with locally downloaded texts.

use_cases.title: Linux Bible Study
use_cases.description: Linux users needing a native, well-integrated Bible study application choose BibleTime over web-only alternatives.

enrichment.short_description: BigDataCloud is an Adelaide, Australia-based API provider offering IP geolocation, reverse geocoding, and network intelligence data for developers and businesses.
enrichment.seo_title: BigDataCloud: IP Geolocation and Network Intelligence API
enrichment.seo_meta_description: BigDataCloud offers IP geolocation, reverse geocoding, and network intelligence APIs with a free tier and usage-based paid plans.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: BigDataCloud is an API provider specializing in IP geolocation, reverse geocoding, and network intelligence data. The company describes its idea as originating in 2016 among founders with backgrounds in network engineering, e-commerce, and fraud prevention, and it was formally established in 2018. BigDataCloud is a small, independent company headquartered in Adelaide, South Australia, with an estimated 1 to 10 employees according to public company data sources. Its core product maps the physical routing structure of the internet, identifying and categorizing public router interfaces and calculating their geographic service areas using verified ground-truth data; the company holds a granted US patent (No. 11,792,110 B2) covering aspects of this router-based geolocation approach. The flagship IP Geolocation API returns location data alongside a confidence area polygon, a set of coordinates representing the realistic geographic boundary within which a given IP address is likely operating, which the company positions as more transparent than services that return only a single point estimate. Related products include a reverse geocoding API and a network-by-IP-address API that identifies ASN and ISP information, and a network intelligence layer that can flag VPNs, proxies, Tor exit nodes, and bot-like traffic. BigDataCloud publishes tiered pricing based on monthly request volume: a free Base plan covering 10,000 requests per month, a Bronze plan at 29.95 dollars per month for 100,000 requests, a Silver plan at 249 dollars per month for 1 million requests, a Gold plan at 1,999 dollars per month for 10 million requests, and a Platinum plan at 9,999 dollars per month for unlimited requests, with overage charges available beyond each plan's included volume. All plans, including the free tier, include access to every API endpoint and response field, GraphQL access, and a stated 99.999 percent uptime SLA on paid plans. The company positions itself as a developer-focused alternative to larger IP intelligence vendors, emphasizing accuracy, transparent pricing, and broad access to functionality across all pricing tiers rather than gating specific fields or endpoints behind higher-priced plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is BigDataCloud
paragraphs: BigDataCloud is an API company focused on IP geolocation, reverse geocoding, and network intelligence data. Its founders describe the idea originating in 2016, with the company formally established in 2018, and it is headquartered in Adelaide, South Australia, as a small, independent team.

The company's core technical approach maps the physical routing structure of the internet, identifying public router interfaces and calculating their geographic service areas from verified ground-truth data, an approach covered by a granted US patent held by BigDataCloud.

BigDataCloud's products are aimed at developers and businesses that need to determine where a user or IP address is located, validate contact and location data, or detect network-level risk signals such as VPNs and proxies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The flagship IP Geolocation API returns not just a location estimate but a confidence area polygon, a set of coordinates outlining the realistic geographic boundary in which a given IP address is likely operating, derived from the company's router service-area analysis.
Beyond geolocation, BigDataCloud offers a reverse geocoding API for converting coordinates into addresses, and a network-by-IP-address API that surfaces ASN and ISP information for a given IP.

Its network intelligence layer can identify VPN, proxy, Tor exit node, and bot-like traffic at the network level, and every plan, including the free tier, provides access to all endpoints and response fields along with GraphQL support for fetching only the specific fields a developer needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: BigDataCloud offers a free Base plan covering 10,000 API requests per month with no credit card required to get started, alongside four paid tiers priced by monthly request volume.

The Bronze plan costs 29.95 dollars per month for 100,000 requests, the Silver plan costs 249 dollars per month for 1 million requests, the Gold plan costs 1,999 dollars per month for 10 million requests, and the Platinum plan costs 9,999 dollars per month for unlimited requests.

All paid plans include the same full set of endpoints and fields as the free tier, GraphQL access, and a stated 99.999 percent SLA, with overage charges available for usage beyond a plan's included request volume.

features.title: IP Geolocation API
features.description: Returns location data for a given IP address, including a confidence area polygon showing the realistic geographic boundary of the result.

features.title: Reverse Geocoding API
features.description: Converts geographic coordinates into human-readable addresses and location descriptions.

features.title: Network-by-IP-Address API
features.description: Identifies ASN and ISP information associated with a given IP address.

features.title: VPN, Proxy, and Tor Detection
features.description: Flags network-level risk signals including VPN usage, proxy connections, and Tor exit nodes.

features.title: Bot Traffic Detection
features.description: Helps identify bot-like traffic patterns at the network level for fraud prevention use cases.

features.title: GraphQL API Access
features.description: All plans include GraphQL access, letting developers request only the specific fields they need in a single query.

features.title: Patented Router-Mapping Technology
features.description: Uses a patented approach (US Patent No. 11,792,110 B2) that maps internet router locations to improve geolocation accuracy.

features.title: Free Tier With Full Feature Access
features.description: The free Base plan includes all API endpoints and fields, not a limited feature subset.

pros: Free tier includes 10,000 requests per month with no credit card required
All pricing tiers include every endpoint and response field
Patented router-mapping approach aims for more transparent, accurate geolocation
Confidence area polygon gives a realistic accuracy boundary rather than a single point estimate
GraphQL support reduces payload size and number of API calls
Published, transparent pricing based on monthly request volume
cons: Small team size may limit support responsiveness for enterprise customers
Higher-volume tiers are priced for businesses with significant request volume
IP-based geolocation is inherently approximate for any provider, including BigDataCloud
Developers unfamiliar with GraphQL face a small learning curve to use it effectively
Overage charges apply once a plan's included request volume is exceeded

faqs.question: Is BigDataCloud free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. BigDataCloud offers a free Base plan covering 10,000 API requests per month with no credit card required to sign up.

faqs.question: How accurate is BigDataCloud's IP geolocation?
faqs.answer: BigDataCloud uses a patented approach that maps public internet routers and their service areas, and returns a confidence area polygon showing the realistic geographic boundary for each result rather than claiming a single exact point.

faqs.question: What is the confidence area polygon?
faqs.answer: It is a set of coordinates forming a closed polygon that represents the realistic boundary within which a given IP address is likely located, based on BigDataCloud's router service-area analysis.

faqs.question: Does BigDataCloud detect VPNs and proxies?
faqs.answer: Yes. Its network intelligence features can flag VPN usage, proxy connections, Tor exit nodes, and bot-like traffic at the network level.

faqs.question: What is included in each BigDataCloud pricing plan?
faqs.answer: Every plan, from the free Base tier through Platinum, includes all API endpoints and response fields; plans differ only by monthly request volume and price.

faqs.question: Does BigDataCloud offer a GraphQL API?
faqs.answer: Yes. All plans include GraphQL access, allowing developers to request specific fields in a single query to reduce payload size.

faqs.question: Where is BigDataCloud based?
faqs.answer: BigDataCloud is headquartered in Adelaide, South Australia, and operates as a small, independent company.

faqs.question: What is BigDataCloud's geolocation patent?
faqs.answer: BigDataCloud holds a granted US patent (No. 11,792,110 B2) covering aspects of its router-mapping approach to IP geolocation.

use_cases.title: Web App Geolocation
use_cases.description: Developers use the IP Geolocation API to localize content, currency, or language based on a visitor's approximate location.

use_cases.title: Fraud Prevention and Cybersecurity
use_cases.description: Security and fraud teams use VPN, proxy, and Tor detection to flag risky sign-ups, logins, or transactions.

use_cases.title: Location-Based Business Features
use_cases.description: Businesses use reverse geocoding and network intelligence data to power location-based features and analytics.
use_cases.audience: Product and data teams building location features
